data.title: Affiliated Managers Group: Solid Investment With Safety From Baby Bonds
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

Today, we present another moderate-risk investment opportunity, focusing on Affiliated Managers Group, Inc. (NYSE:AMG) and its baby bonds: (NYSE:MGR), (NYSE:MGRE), (NYSE:MGRD), and (NYSE:MGRB).

Company overview

Founded in 1993, Affiliated Managers Group, Inc. is based in West Palm Beach, Florida, and manages approximately $8.8 billion in assets. The company has a market capitalization of close to $7 billion. The company partners with entrepreneurial, growing independent investment firms, providing access to specialized opportunities at scale. AMG falls into the “investment grade” category with an A3 rating from Moody’s and BBB+ from S&P.

The common stock is traded at a price of $244.34 currently:

Let's look at a comparison between AMG and SPY:

for 1-year total return:

3-year total return:

5-year total return:

In all cases, AMG outperforms at the moment.

AMG has maintained 7 years of continuous dividend payments with a yield of 0.02% and a low payout ratio of 0.18%. Here's what the yield for the last year looks like:

Baby bonds

AMG's baby bonds have a long duration (33–38 years to maturity) and are investment grade, rated BBB- by S&P and Baa1 by Moody's:

(MGRB) is trading at $18.88, below par, with a yield to maturity of 6.80%. The maturity date is 09/30/2060 (35.03 years to maturity), and the call date is 09/30/2025 (0.03 years). It pays 4.75% interest per annum, or $1.1875 ($0.296875 per quarter). 10 million notes were issued on 09/17/2020.

At the time of writing, (MGR) is priced at $22.78, below par, with a yield to maturity of 6.80%. The maturity date is on 03/30/2059, and the call date expired on 03/30/2024. It pays 5.88% interest per annum, or $1.46875, in the form of $0.3671875 per quarter. 11.2 million notes were issued on 03/22/2019.

(MGRD)is currently trading at $16.66, below par, with a yield to maturity of 6.92%. Maturity date is on 09/30/2061, and the call date is 09/30/2025 (with 1.03 years to call). It pays 4.20% interest per annum, or $1.05 ($0.2625 per quarter). 8 million notes were issued on 07/08/2021.

At the moment, (MGRE) is priced $25.06, above par. The maturity date is on 03/30/2064, with 38.53 years remaining and a yield to maturity of 7.02%, and the call date is on 03/30/2029 (3.53 years to call), with a yield to call of 7.27%. It pays 6.75% interest per year, or $1.6875 ($0.421875 per quarter). 16 million notes were issued on 03/1/2024.

Disclosure: For those wondering how we calculate the yields, we calculate the internal rate of return for a series of cash flows. We include an initial investment (or current price), periodic payments, and a final payment at maturity, which allows us to calculate XIRR.

Below is shown a comparison between MGR, MGRD, and MGRB ( the AMG's baby bonds that trade below par), and their peers in the finance sector, investment grade, traded below par. We can see AMG's bonds are among the highest yields and highest credit ratings:

For MGRE and its peers, the bonds that trade above par, the thing is the same - AMG's bond is in the top:

OTC bonds

AMG's over-the-counter bonds have an A3 rating from Moody's, slightly higher than that of the baby bonds:

Their yield range is from 4.3% to 5.07%, but we prefer a notch down credit rating and a much higher yield.

Risks

Investing in AMG's baby bonds offers high yields of 6.80–7.02%, but carries risks. Changes in interest rates can affect bond prices. Long maturities and high duration expose investors to long-term market risks. Market volatility in the financial sector and AMG's dependence on the performance of its investment partner firms can also affect returns.

Conclusion

AMG offers an attractive investment opportunity and a stable business model. Its baby bonds with a yield of 6.8–7.02% and investment grade rating provide high income with moderate risk, although their long maturities bring market volatility.

data.title: The Real Winner Of Rate Cuts
data.description:
Introduction

One of the core economic issues that the US is having to confront is the national debt—the extent to which you believe we are or are not addressing it may vary wildly, as it is a rather polarizing issue.

As a country, we are yet again at very serious levels, with alarm bells going off, as the Treasury is set to issue another $1.6T in debt for H2 2025.

While every administration since Clinton in the late 90s has operated in an annual deficit, and no administration since Kennedy in the early 60s has had no debt at all, the current administration inherited one of the hotter potatoes to be passed in recent memory, currently at $37T.

Projections for the next ten years are fairly grim; that trendline is steep.

There are many contributing factors, and their actual contributions to the issue remain speculative (economic fog of war), but here's my take:

  • Revenue is well below expenses, and revenue is now being cut in some places like corporate, payroll, and income taxes, traditionally the largest contributors of Treasury receipts. Here is the cumulative year through August for receipts and outlays.
  • Despite cuts to expenses being made by the current administration, overall spending increases in other categories (i.e., defense) have led to a net increase in government spending.
  • The previous administration blundered an opportunity to refinance debt at very low rates for the long term, leaving the current administration to refill the Treasury accounts with much more expensive debt.

Having to borrow at rates above 4% is tough for the government because of the sheer amount of debt they have and what they have rolling over. This has led to the current administration issuing an abundance of short-term debt, intended to be rolled over when rates are lower in the coming months.

The current administration is also resorting to some unique tactics to generate revenue, but it doesn't look like it will be enough revenue to offset the deficit. The net interest snowball is seemingly too big for them to stop; tariff revenue ("customs duties" above) is at an all-time high collection rate, and it is a pittance compared to the amount we spend on interest payments alone, which totals more than double what we spend (Federally) on education and veterans' benefits combined.

A saving grace for the Treasury is that borrowing rates are looking like they are on the downtrend.

Rates Are Coming Down

Earlier this week, we got our first Fed Funds Rate cut in a hot minute, a "risk management cut," as Powell called it. The meeting was more hawkish than I expected, but the long and short of it is that we should expect one or (more likely) two more rate cuts this year, leaving the lower limit of the rate around 3.5% by Jan. 2026. At least, for now. That may change as time goes on.

There's a lot of speculation about what will benefit from rate cuts, especially if we know that they are coming. I've read a ton of compelling arguments around real estate, gold, long-term treasury ETFs, and stocks, but I think a lot of this commentary misses the real winner: the US Treasury itself.

Going further, I think that the US Treasury (and the Fed) has a good shot at taming the 10-year rate and easing the strain placed on the government by its borrowing.

The Treasury Buyback Program

With rates still elevated at the long end, but rates coming down at the short end of the yield curve, the Treasury has an interesting option available to it: it can use its buyback program, in conjunction with some Fed magic—as discussed in this great piece from Carlyle—to retire debt at a faster rate than it has to issue it. Essentially, this means that the Treasury could buy its debt back at a discount and issue new debt at face value, pocketing the difference (which would be shaved off the debt pile).

The Treasury Buyback Program has been running smoothly so far in its implementation and is ripe for expansion into a program like this as soon as late next year.

Carlyle gives a great explanation of how the Treasury may use this program to its advantage and reduce the net debt:

In July [2025], Treasury announced its intention to keep fixed-rate (notes and bonds) auction sizes constant in the coming quarters and instead rely on short-term debt (bills) to cover incremental financing needs...Under reasonable assumptions, bills could account for more than 25% of the outstanding stock of Treasury debt, up from a historic average of roughly 14% and 20% currently...When accounting for the notes and bonds set to mature, $12 trillion of nominal Treasury debt could (re) price over the next 16 months...If rates fall to levels consistent with Administration preferences, Treasury could save $200 billion (0.7% of GDP) in annual interest expense...

...Additional interest savings could come through a recently-expanded buyback program that authorizes the repurchase of $38 billion of outstanding Treasury securities each quarter...As a result of the sharp increase in interest rates since 2022, the market value of the stock of Treasury debt is worth only about $94 per $100 of face value...on average, with 30-year bonds recently trading at just $67 per $100 of par...This implies that $100 of new bill issuance could retire as much as $150 of outstanding debt. With half of the buybacks targeting the (more discounted) 10-to-30-year portion of the curve, Treasury could cut the outstanding stock of debt by nearly $10 billion every three months.

But there’s a catch. Unless short-term interest rates fall, Treasury’s net interest expense could rise as a result of these transactions. (That should be no surprise; those discounts exist precisely because that outstanding debt pays really low coupons!) At current rates, the annualized interest expense on $100 of 3-month bills amounts to $4.15, which exceeds the interest currently paid on the $150 of 30-year bonds ($3.73) or $113 of 10-year notes ($2.12) those new bills would be used to retire...Fed cooperation is thus essential...

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent is too clever to pass this opportunity up, and the administration could very well get away with a fiscal maneuver this size, so long as the Fed plays along. That's looking less likely now than it was a week ago, given the discipline that the Fed showed at this last meeting. However, all bets are off when Chair Powell is replaced in May 2026, as a new captain will likely be a dove, in line with President Trump's (who nominates the chair, pending Senate approval) view of monetary policy, which he has been very vocal about.

Cooperation from the Fed would be very likely at that point, given that other doves such as Governors Bowman (no relation), Waller, and Miran (who may be replaced or appointed full-time by this point) have commented that they would be open to such moves taken by the Fed, with Miran the most vocal.

It may make sense for the Fed, too, who can offload some of the losses they've been incurring while aiding the fiscal side of things indirectly.

Implications & Conclusion

Assuming that rates continue on their current course, I would expect the rates on cash to slowly erode, which will drive up liquidity. This will lower the rate that T-bills and other short-term debt pay but may not wholly affect the longer end of the curve. While the long end typically falls during times of low short-end rates, we are currently seeing long-end rates defy Fed cuts.

See the last two years of yield curves around this time. Despite short-term rates being higher in 2023 (green) and 2024 (red), the 20-year and 30-year bonds are paying higher rates in 2025 (blue). That is not a good sign, as it means bond traders are pricing in more future inflation now than they were in the past. Our curve is reverting but steepening, with the 10-year as a pivot point.

While I'm unsure about the longest end, I do see control being established over the 10-year, as is the focus of the talk around yield curve control that Governor Miran has introduced to the conversation as of the last meeting. This will take months to play out, as this plan from the Treasury and Fed is not in action yet.

Traction isn't there yet, and it may be a slow process. If the next chair is a believer in a 3rd Fed mandate, meaning that the Fed has an equal obligation to the 10-year treasury rate as it does to unemployment and stable prices (the current Fed says that stable 10-year rates are a passive byproduct of achieving the dual mandate), they could easily use the tools at their disposal to bring the 10-year rate down, as discussed in the previous section. That would mean an opportunity in the price of the 10-year, as it would rise while rates fall.

While an ETF that owns those intermediate-term bonds is a consideration, like IEF, my tool of choice is the Simplify Bond Bull ETF (RFIX). It acts as a seven-year expiry call option on the 10-year treasury, a bet that its yield will drop significantly, but can be held in a portfolio indefinitely due to its very long expiration and mandate to roll to prevent theta decay from significantly affecting the fund.

I wrote about my position in RFIX back in June, and it has taken a slight beating since then, as the bond market has tried to defy the Fed. However, it's looking increasingly like the Treasury and the Fed are willing and able to use the tools at hand to fight back against the bond market and subdue the 10-year yield. I expect them to continue making headway against the 10-year with each subsequent rate cut—there are likely two more this year. That is a major bull thesis for RFIX and a call to investors that want to lock in preferable long-term rates in treasuries that we may be seeing a relative peak in rates.

I doubt rates on the longest end (30-year) go up meaningfully from here, with 5% acting as a firm barrier for now. If someone asked me if today was the right time to lock in and buy 20-year Treasury Bonds, I'd tell them there hasn't been a better time in recent history, and it's looking to me like the window of now being a good time is fleeting.

Make of that (and this chart) what you will.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Scholastic Corporation: It's Wise To Be Cautious
data.description:
September 19th was a painful day for shareholders of Scholastic Corporation (NASDAQ:SCHL). Shares of the company, which plays in the publishing space, dropped over 12% at one point after management announced financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. In addition to financial performance worsening on a year-over-year basis, the results reported by management came in lower than what analysts were anticipating. Even though the quarter was very painful, led by significant weakness in its Education Solutions and Entertainment segments, management believes that this year will be positive on the whole.

In the past, I have taken a more neutral stance on the business. But admittedly, it has been quite a while since I last dug into it. My last article on the company was published in August of 2021. And in that time since, shares of the firm have dropped by 23.7%, while the S&P 500 is up 48.2%. The overall financial trajectory of the business from a revenue perspective has been solid. But there is no doubt that bottom-line results or suffering. While shares of the company are cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms, I do think investors would be wise to remain cautious. In the event that management can pull off improved financial performance, the upside could be meaningful. But the risks look relatively high to the point where the most generous I can be is to keep it rated a Hold.

It's wise to be cautious

Fundamentally speaking, the first quarter of 2026 was not a good time for shareholders of Scholastic. Revenue, for instance, came in at $225.6 million. That represents a decline of 4.9% compared to the $237.2 million the company reported the previous year. The revenue reported by the firm also ended up being $13.3 million lower than what analysts were hoping to see. To be clear, not every part of the business experienced weakness during this time. Take, for instance, the Children’s Book Publishing and Distribution segment. Overall revenue actually increased from $105.4 million to $109.4 million.

The big driver of this increase in revenue involved Book Fairs, with sales jumping 18.4% from $28.8 million to $34.1 million. Management claimed that this was because of higher redemptions of Scholastic Dollars, which is a reward currency that the company issues under these operations. This meant greater engagement from its Book Fairs hosts. This is an area of the company that management has been working on improving. For instance, they have been pushing for more larger fairs and been working on ways to reduce churn. And according to management, bookings for this Fall are "strong and ahead of last year's bookings." Even bottom-line results improved, with the firm's operating loss for this segment contracting slightly from $36.6 million to $35.1 million.

Another growth area for the company involved its International segment, with revenue climbing from $56.8 million last year to $59.4 million this year. Management claimed that this increase largely came from Australia, the UK, and Asia. According to management, the company continues to focus on rationalizing its international portfolio and focusing on other ways to boost margins. It appears as though one key opportunity for it would be English as a second language programs in major growing markets like India and the Philippines. And the margin improvement efforts that the company has made here have improved the operating loss for the segment from $8.3 million last year to $4.2 million this year.

Unfortunately, the other segments for the company experienced pain. Chief among these was the Education Solutions segment, which reported a plunge in revenue from $55.7 million to $40.1 million. Apparently, increased uncertainty when it comes to funding for schools and school districts has had a deleterious impact on spending when it comes to supplemental curriculum materials. This is understandable. I also have to wonder to what extent changes in the preparation and consumption of educational materials might affect the company in light of AI. As revenue for this segment plummeted, its operating loss worsened from $17 million to $21.2 million.

Lastly, we have the Entertainment segment, which reported a decline in revenue from $16.6 million to $13.6 million. The good news is that this likely is a one time thing. I say that because, based on the data management has provided, there were anticipated delays in production green lights that the company experienced. On the other hand, costs worsened, with the company booking a $2.5 million intangible amortization associated with its 9 Story transaction, which was up from the $1.8 million reported the previous year. This was instrumental in pushing the operating loss for the segment up from $0.5 million to $4 million.

With revenue for the company declining, profits and cash flows mostly worsened. The firm's loss per share, for instance, widened from $2.21 to $2.83. This caused it to miss analysts’ expectations by $0.39 per share and resulted in the firm’s overall net loss growing from $62.5 million to $71.1 million. Adjusted earnings per share, meanwhile, fell from negative $2.13 to negative $2.52. That ended up causing the firm to miss expectations by $0.11 per share and led to a worsening in adjusted net loss from $60.3 million to $63.3 million. Operating cash flow worsened from negative $41.9 million to negative $81.8 million. I normally like to strip out changes in working capital as well to give an adjusted figure. But management has not released enough details to make that possible. Finally, we did see a bit of improvement when it came to EBITDA, with that going from negative $60.5 million to negative $55.7 million.

Over the last few years now, Scholastic has experienced a rather bumpy ride. For instance, revenue for the company dropped from $1.70 billion in 2023 to $1.59 billion in 2024. But then in the 2025 fiscal year, there was a rise to $1.63 billion. This improvement came largely because of a surge in Entertainment revenue from $1.9 million to $61 million, with that increase caused by the company's purchase of 9 Story Media Group in a deal valued at $176.2 million on a net basis. However, both the Children’s Book Publishing and Distribution segment and the International segment reported growth also. The pain point ended up being the Education Solutions segment, with revenue falling from $351.2 million to $309.8 million. This, according to management, was caused by school districts adopting and implementing new evidence-based core programs and reducing spending on supplemental materials. There were other weak spots as well, such as a decline in subscription revenue for Magazines+ and lower revenues from community literacy programs.

The choppiness in revenue is one thing. But even more problematic has been pain on the bottom line. From 2023 through 2024, the company went from a net profit of $86.3 million to a net loss of $1.9 million. Even adjusted net income plummeted from $86.3 million to $13.3 million. Interestingly, the company's cost of goods sold actually improved markedly, declining from $786.4 million to $718.8 million over the last three fiscal years. The problem, then, was chiefly related to a surge in selling, general, and administrative expenses from $756.6 million to $822.3 million. Certain restructuring and cost-savings initiatives, as well as the purchase of 9 Story, seem to be the culprits here. And as these bottom-line results worsened, cash flow metrics declined as well.

The good news is that management seems to be optimistic about the near term. Thanks to continued cost-savings initiatives, as well as the expectation that overall revenue this year should be higher by between 2% and 4%, the belief is that EBITDA will range between $160 million and $170 million. At the midpoint, that would represent an increase of 13.5% compared to the $145.4 million the company reported the previous year. In all likelihood, this will mean adjusted operating cash flow of around $144.9 million.

In the event that these estimates come to fruition, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. Honestly, these are really low trading multiples. And they would normally be indicative of a company that is significantly undervalued. In addition to this, the company is also cheap compared to other similar firms. In the table below, I stacked it up against five such enterprises. What I found was that, across the board, Scholastic is cheaper than any of these.

Company Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Scholastic Corporation 4.1 5.1
Pearson (PSO) 6.9 N/A
John Wiley & Sons (WLY) 10.9 7.6
News Corp (NWS) 16.9 13.5
Gannett (GCI) 7.0 8.1
The New York Times Company (NYT) 19.8 17.7

Takeaway

As a value investor, it's always tempting to jump into a firm that is trading on the cheap. And the fact of the matter is that shares of Scholastic are some of the cheapest of any shares that I have seen in a while. Its relative valuation is also attractive, and management's expectations for the rest of this year are encouraging. Having said that, the firm has had a bumpy track record, and it's clear that the market is not confident that management will be able to achieve these targets after what I can only describe was a very painful quarterly release. Until we have more clarity, I think the best thing I can do is to rate the firm a Hold for now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Xencor: Bispecific Antibody Program Takes Shape With Q4 Data Release
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Xencor (NASDAQ:XNCR), it was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Xencor: Plamotamab NHL Data Leads To RA Program Advancement." With respect to this article, I noted the opportunity that the company had in developing the use of its bispecific CD20 X CD3 antibody for the treatment of patients with rheumatoid arthritis [RA]. This program is moving forward in a phase 1b/2a study as of the latest progress. What I also had the last time around was a "Strong Buy" rating on this company on the basis of advancing its pipeline full of bispecific antibodies for various disorders. I am still going to keep this rating on this stock for now because the company continues to make progress in terms of advancing several different types of XmAb antibodies. XmAb942 is a potent, extended half-life anti-TL1A antibody being developed to target patients with inflammatory disorders.

What must be known right away, though, is that this particular drug is a monospecific antibody that deploys a different Fc region known as Xtend Fc Domain. To that end, there is a catalyst opportunity here. It is expected that the company is going to initiate the phase 2b XENITH-UC trial, which is going to evaluate the use of XmAb942 for the treatment of patients with Ulcerative Colitis [UC]. Such a mid-stage trial is expected to be initiated in the 2nd half of 2025. It recently reported positive interim data from this program as well, showing that the drug was well tolerated with no serious adverse events [SAEs]. In addition, it was noted that free circulating TL1A was reduced substantially in the patient-treated cohorts compared to the placebo cohort.

This time around, I believe it is important to highlight an entirely different candidate that Xencor is working on. This would be with the development of its bispecific ENPP3 X CD3 antibody XmAb819 for the treatment of patients with relapsed/refractory clear cell renal cell carcinoma [ccRCC]. The use of this drug is being explored for the treatment of these patients in an ongoing open-label phase 1 study. The reason why I want to focus on this program specifically is because there is an inflection point for this program. The company believes that it is going to be able to release preliminary phase 1 data at a medical meeting in Q4 of 2025.

The truth is that the stock price has declined by 60% since the last SA article I wrote on it. I think that it was the lack of near-term catalysts that caused the decline and nothing bad with the biotech itself. However, I don't think people appreciate the upcoming preliminary data to be released from the phase 1 study using XmAb819 for the treatment of patients with relapsed/refractory clear cell renal cell carcinoma [ccRCC]. The point I'm trying to get at is that this newly released data could end up reinforcing the other prior data that was released for it. In other words, it would serve as further confirmation. A second reason why I'm bullish despite the decline is that the company has the potential to maybe have a solid monospecific anti-TL1A antibody. Why would this be the case? It is because the company incorporates its Xtend Fc domain technology to improve the half-life of this drug. If this advantage and other advantages are laid out in new data releases, then there is a chance for it to have a superior drug in this specific TL1A class. I believe that the stock market is missing these attributes of it, particularly the company's XmAb protein engineering platform.

XmAb819 Progress Continues With Initial Data Release At Upcoming Medical Conference

As I noted above, Xencor is making significant progress in terms of the development of several of its drugs. A highly promising one would be continued advancement of monospecific anti-TL1A antibody XmAb942 for the treatment of patients with inflammatory disorders like UC. However, I believe it is important to see if its 2+1 bispecific antibody with Fc domain XmAb819 is able to help treat patients with relapsed/refractory ccRCC. Before going over this phase 1 study program in detail, plus any catalysts that are to be released here, I believe that it is first important to go over what this type of cancer is and what market opportunity could be possible for the company. Renal cell carcinoma [RCC] is characterized as the growth of malignant cells on the kidney of a patient. Specifically, cancer cells invade the inner lining tubes of the kidney, which in turn causes major problems for a patient. The downside is that in the early stages of disease there are hardly any symptoms, but when they are present, they could be one or more of the following:

  • Pain the back part of a person's body
  • Blood found in the urine
  • Weight loss that occurs randomly
  • Fatigue

Even though symptoms are not present in these patients, this type of cancer is still found because of extensive testing. In particular, with more CT scan tests being done, this cancer can easily be discovered. The global renal cancer drugs market is projected to reach $8 billion by 2027. This is a very big market opportunity, but it is important to note that the company has a focus on targeting clear cell patients. This would still be a big market opportunity for the company. Why do I make such a claim? It is because the histological clear cell type accounts for 90% of all RCC tumors. The hope is that the company's drug can help treat these patients effectively.

In order to determine whether or not XmAb819 is going to be effective in being able to treat these patients with advanced clear cell renal cell carcinoma [ccRCC], it is in the process of evaluating this 2 + 1 bispecific antibody in an ongoing open-label phase 1 study. The purpose of this trial is to assess either the use of intravenous [IV] or subcutaneous [SC] dosing of XmAb819. This trial is expected to recruit up to a total of 95 patients and evaluate them primarily for safety, pharmacokinetic [PK], and pharmacodynamic [PD] profile. Along with the recommended dose as well. It is important to note that this trial is expected to be broken into two different parts. One part, Part A, is the dose-escalation phase of this trial. Then, Part B is going to be looking at dose expansion instead. This is not going to be an easy population to treat using XmAb819.

The reason why I state this is because these are patients with relapsed/refractory disease. In essence, they were previously treated with standard of care [SOC] therapy. The good news is that the company already released some progress that had been made with the development of this drug. For starters, despite starting off with the dose escalation portion of this phase 1 study, it was noted that there was anti-tumor activity present. Why is this significant? That's because another finding was that the maximum tolerated dose [MTD] was not yet found. In essence, with anti-tumor activity already being noted, there is room to improve in terms of efficacy with higher doses of XmAb819. Of course, it is important for a therapy to be effective. However, not only is progress being made in regard to this, but it is also noted that there is a sufficient duration of response [DOR].

Basically, patients in certain cohorts are reaching long-duration response periods of more than one year. What makes this drug ideal is the nature of deploying the 2 + 1 design. The reason why is because this allows XmAb819 to be selectively potent to ENPP3 tumor-expressing cells and not healthy tissue cells. Remember above? Where I stated that ccRCC accounts for 90% of all RCC cases. Well, there is a similar targeting method in terms of biomarker expression. That is, the drug is developed to target ENPP3 expression, and as such, this biomarker type is said to be found in 90% of ccRCC cases. This is where a catalyst opportunity comes into play. The company believes that it will be able to share initial data from this phase 1 study, using XmAb819 for the treatment of patients with advanced RCC, at a medical presentation meeting in Q4 of 2025.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Xencor had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable debt securities of $663.8 million as of June 30, 2025. The reason for the cash on hand is because the company had utilized several financial tools to date to keep its operations going. What makes this biotech ideal is that it has plenty of cash on hand. Why do I make such a claim? It is because in its 10-Q SEC filing, it notes that it believes its cash on hand is going to be enough to fund its operations into 2028. This means it has plenty of cash to continue to fund itself for an extended period of time.

It likely won't need to raise any funds for the time being, but just in case it needs to, there is a financial option at its disposal to use. This would be in terms of an open market sale agreement that that company had done. This was an ATM offering agreement where it could from time to time offer and sell up to $200 million worth of shares of its common stock. As of June 30, 2025, it has not yet sold any shares under this Sales Agreement. The company burns roughly $76.8 million in cash per quarter. This is broken down into $61.7 million in R&D expenses and then $15.1 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Xencor. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the development of its bispecific ENPP3 X CD3 antibody XmAb819 for the treatment of patients with advanced ccRCC, which is being explored in a phase 1 study. Preliminary data from this early-stage trial is expected to be presented at an upcoming medical conference in Q4 of 2025. The risk here is that even though the company released some encouraging findings from this phase 1 study, like initial anti-tumor activity and no MTD being reached, there is no assurance that additional positive results will be released at the upcoming medical conference.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the clinical development of XmAb942 as a potential best-in-class anti-TL1A therapy to treat patients with inflammatory disorders. The goal is to use this drug to treat patients with moderate-to-severe UC who had progressed on prior therapy in the phase 2b XENITH-UC study. The risk here is that there is no assurance that upon completion of such a study, the results will turn out to be positive. Another risk to consider would be the competitive nature of this space. By this, I mean there is a lot of competition in this class of drug space. Just to name a few of them, there would be Merck (MRK) with MK-7240 and Roche (RHHYB) with RG6631. There are many others, but the point is that such drugs could end up competing with XmAb942. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the primary endpoint to be laid out for the phase 2b study is going to be achieved in a statistically significant manner. Nor that the data to be produced will be enough for the company to warrant further investigation of this bispecific 2 + 1 antibody in a phase 3 clinical study. The positive is that proof-of-concept has been established with anti-TL1A drugs in the treatment of patients with inflammatory disorders like UC and Crohn's Disease [CD]. The downside is that Xencor, with its drug XmAb81, will need to have some type of competitive edge over these other drugs of the same class. For instance, superior safety, dosing schedule, or efficacy, but this remains to be seen for now.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of an expansion opportunity. This relates to the development of another drug candidate that is able to target two cytokine targets for potential superior efficacy, which is a TL1A x IL23p19 bispecific antibody. The reason for such a candidate is to use the simple bispecific format of immunoglobulin G [IgG] instead of a monospecific one. In essence, the bispecific nature of this drug has been able to engage in similar target potency in in vitro testing compared to that of monospecific antibodies. If preclinical testing goes well, then the company should be able to initiate a phase 1 study using this TL1A x IL13p19 bispecific antibody in 2026.

Conclusion

Xencor has done well to continue to build its pipeline. I believe that it has an excellent opportunity in terms of developing its anti-TL1A monospecific antibody XmAb942 to target patients with inflammatory disorders. There are two hurdles that it must overcome with respect to the advancement of this program. The first of which would be in terms of finding some competitive edge, throughout its clinical trials process. As I have stated above, the TL1A drug class space is highly crowded, and without some type of competitive edge, the company won't do well. Secondly, it needs to achieve the primary endpoint of its expected phase 2b study of XENITH-UC. This particular trial is expected to target patients with moderate-to-severe UC and is expected to be initiated in the 2nd half of 2025.

The risk here is that there is no assurance that the primary endpoint of clinical remission of the modified Mayo score at week 12 is going to be met in a statistically significant manner. The thing is that XmAb942 is only monospecific, and Xencor does not just have this type of antibody as part of its pipeline. Most notably, it has a pipeline full of 2 + 1 bispecific antibodies that it is working on. With one of the bispecific antibodies I went over above being XmAb819. This particular clinical candidate is being explored in a phase 1 study to target relapsed/refractory clear cell renal cell carcinoma [ccRCC] patients. The importance of this is that it could further add proof-of-concept that the company's deployment of two tumor antigen-binding domains and one T-cell binding domain.

The two-domain nature of XmAb819 is important because it gives two domains a chance to have greater selectivity in tissue binding to reduce toxicity. The hope is that the company can report positive initial data from the ongoing phase 1 study, but this remains to be seen. With Q4 of 2025 presentation of dose-escalation data from the phase 1 study using XmAb819 to target patients with ccRCC, plus continued advancement of its very own monospecific, half-life extended anti-TL1A antibody, I believe that it is important to keep a "Strong Buy" rating on this stock. Should the company report weak data in Q4 of 2025, which warrants either having to increase the dose of Xmab819 or remove the program entirely, then I would consider changing the rating thereafter.

data.title: Lyft: Waymo Is A Trojan Horse, I'm Out (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Lyft (NASDAQ:LYFT) stock has surged as of late in part due to news it would be partnering with autonomous vehicle leader Waymo (GOOGL) (GOOG) in Nashville. This announcement was probably most surprising due to the (now incorrect) investor perception that the company’s bigger competitor would be the main partner in the country. The company has seen sustained GAAP profitability and is seeing momentum in building partnerships. The stock is still not that expensive, but I view the recent strength as an opportunity to take profits. I am downgrading the stock to a "Neutral" rating.

LYFT Stock Price

I last covered LYFT in March, where I called the stock “deep value” because of GAAP profitability. The stock has roared 84% higher since then.

I am concerned that the reasons driving this surge might be the catalysts behind tail-end risk.

LYFT Stock Key Metrics

LYFT is a global rideshare company. While larger competitor Uber (UBER) appears to have been successful in winning the lion’s share of market share, LYFT has capitalized on winning key partnerships to stay relevant. Whereas UBER has partnered with American Express (AXP), LYFT has partnered with Chase (JPM). Whereas UBER has their own food delivery services, LYFT has also partnered with DoorDash (DASH).

In the most recent quarter, LYFT saw active riders show sequential growth after declining sequentially in the first quarter. I note that this is likely in part due to the summer season.

The company generated 12% YoY growth in gross bookings to $4.49 billion, coming in between guidance of between $4.41 billion and $4.57 billion.

The company generated $129 million in adjusted EBITDA, coming at the high end of guidance of $115 million to $130 million.

I note that the company saw its GAAP operating margin turn positive.

The company also generated positive GAAP net income for the third straight quarter. I expect the company to maintain GAAP profitability moving forward.

LYFT ended the quarter with $1.8 billion of cash versus $526 million of debt, representing a solid balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management guided the third quarter to see a substantial acceleration in growth, with gross bookings expected to grow by as much as 17% YoY.

On the conference call, management was optimistic about how autonomous vehicles may prove to be a tailwind for the company, noting that markets with AVs are “5x larger” than those without. Management suggested that integrating AVs could lead to favorable economics, as the reduced operational costs (which include expensive insurance) would more than offset lower sticker prices, with the latter also potentially fueling greater app use.

Is LYFT Stock a Buy, Sell, or Hold?

The real big news as of late was that LYFT would be partnering with Waymo in Nashville. This was significant because it is their first large-scale partnership. This throws a wrench in the UBER bull case, at least on the notion that UBER would be the primary beneficiary of Waymo’s rollout.

After the surge, LYFT stock traded hands at around 1.4x sales, still representing a sizable discount to the 3.8x multiple at UBER.

I have made the case that the relative discount was too large in the past. With the discount having narrowed considerably, it bears noting that some relative discount makes sense given that UBER is likely to always have superior profit margins due to the larger scale. Assuming LYFT can achieve 10% net margins over the long term, that places the valuation at around 13x long-term earnings. That valuation looks at least reasonable, assuming the company can hit consensus estimates of double-digit top-line growth moving forward. That said, I suspect that valuation multiples might compress over the long term as Waymo inevitably builds out a direct-to-consumer relationship. If Waymo really does help power growth at LYFT and UBER, then I would expect it to eventually seek to drive riders to its own app. I do not buy arguments about a moat at existing ridesharing companies. GOOGL will be able to use its existing Google Maps network, and I believe that “Waymo” already has as much name-brand recognition as the incumbent ridesharing providers. While the stock might see momentum in the near to medium term if growth and profits rise due to the AV momentum, I suspect that the stock is poised to underperform the broader market over the long term.

LYFT Stock Conclusion

This is one of my harder calls to make, as the company is finally showing the momentum that I have been waiting for. Yet investors should not overlook the rapid rise of Waymo and others, nor should they ignore the likelihood that Waymo emerges as a competitor rather than a partner. With the stock trading “not that cheaply,” I must choose caution over greed. I instead recommend investors to search for undervalued opportunities among quality names, as I have done in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am downgrading the stock to a "Neutral" rating.

data.title: Lundin Gold: Zero Margin Of Safety At Current Levels
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless a C$ or A$ is in front of the figure. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day. UG = Underground. OP = open-pit. All production figures are on an attributable basis unless otherwise noted.

Q2 Production & Sales

Lundin Gold released its Q2’25 results last month, reporting quarterly gold production of ~139,400 ounces (+5% year-over-year). Higher production was driven by increased tonnes mined/processed post plant expansion and higher recoveries (addition of Jameson cells). As for grades, head grades fell to 10.4 G/T of gold (Q2’24: 11.0 G/T of gold), in line with mine sequencing and what was expected to be the higher grade portion of the year.

Given the solid year-to date performance, Lundin Gold tightened its guidance range to 490,000 to 525,000 ounces (previously 475,000 to 525,000 ounces), with ~52% of its guidance midpoint delivered to date. Unfortunately, it will not have strong grades to lean on in H2’25 with management noting that grades are expected to drop below 9 G/T of gold in H2-25, suggesting up to a 15% dip in grades from H1 to H2.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

"Sure, Ron. Yes, we're looking at grades between 9 and 10 grams for the balance of the year, closer to 9, I would say."

- Lundin Gold, Q2'25 Conference Call

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

That said, assuming ~5,300 TPD processing rates, 90.6% recoveries and a lower head grade of 8.6 G/T of gold for conservatism, the implied ~243,000 ounces in H2 would still place Lundin Gold just above its guidance midpoint, allowing it to deliver its fourth consecutive best on guidance. Further, given Lundin Gold’s flawless track record of over-delivering on promises under CEO Ron Hochstein, I would not be surprised to see a beat on the ~501,000 ounces implied by the above metrics.

As for how Lundin Gold will nearly match H1’25 production levels despite milling lower grades, the company hinted that its hope is to reach its 2026 target of 5,500 TPD by January 1st, 2026, suggesting that the company could be processing at much higher rates in the second half of Q4’25 to offset the lower grade profile.

Finally, regarding maintaining a ~500,000 ounce production profile despite declining grades in its LOMP (ex-FDNS), Lundin Gold noted it could look at an incremental expansion down the road to 6,000 to 6,500 TPD and that there might still be more upside from a recovery standpoint. Overall, this commentary combined with another quarter of phenomenal execution is certainly positive, confirming that FDN should be able to maintain 500,000 ounce production out to the end of the decade and helping to maintain the stock's premium multiple.

Looking at Lundin Gold’s financial results, revenue hit a new quarterly record at ~$453 million and operating cash flow surged to ~$255 million, with ~$236 million in free cash flow generated in Q2’25. This allowed Lundin Gold to exit with over $490 million in net cash and supported another massive dividend payment of $0.79/share coming to shareholders at the end of Q3’25 based on its fixed and variable dividend.

It's important to note that this was accomplished at a $3,361/oz gold price and while AISC could come in closer to $1,050/oz in the next two quarters, we should still see considerable free cash flow generation in H2'25.

Costs & Margins

As for cost performance and margins, Lundin Gold reported Q2’25 AISC of $925/oz (+6% year-over-year), impacted by higher royalties and profit sharing (gold price related) that were out of its control and higher sustaining capital year-over-year. These results allowed Lundin Gold to maintain its position as one of the lowest cost precious metals producers, even more impressive given that it’s not benefiting from considerable by-product credits to drag costs lower. That said, Q2’25 AISC benefited from what will be back-end weighted sustaining capital spend this year, with just ~29% of planned sustaining capital spent in H1’25 thus far, improving overall AISC performance in the period.

Hence, while H1’25 AISC might imply a beat vs. its guidance midpoint, this is not likely to be the case. This is because Lundin Gold will have to contend with higher royalties (stronger gold price) and significant catch-up on sustaining capital, pushing AISC up vs. H1-25 levels. The miss vs. its guidance midpoint is not a big deal and even keeping costs below $1,000/oz for the full year would be exceptional given that it guided based on a $2,500/oz gold price, creating a $90/oz headwind to delivering on costs ($1,055/oz vs. $965/oz midpoint) assuming a $3,400/oz average price this year ($10/oz escalation per $100/oz increase in gold price).

In summary, anything below $1,035/oz for FY2025 AISC is a beat given that the cost impacts were entirely out of Lundin Gold’s control. As for margin performance, AISC margins soared to $2,434/oz, crushing its past record and increasing to 72.4% or 920 basis points year-over-year. These are near royalty/streaming company like margins and are truly phenomenal, a testament to the world-class nature of this orebody and the team’s commitment to seek out continuous improvements on site. Let’s take a look at recent developments.

Recent Developments

As for recent developments, we have yet to see any activity on the M&A front for Lundin Gold in what’s been a very busy period for the sector. This is despite the company working with exceptionally strong currency (share price) and trading at a material premium to diversified peers that would certainly help with the accretiveness of a deal with LUG positioned similarly to KL in 2019 with a beefy production profile, industry-leading margins, strong currency, but high NAV concentration to a single asset.

In defense of the lack of M&A activity, Lundin Gold has seen a massive improvement in its organic growth pipeline with new porphyry discoveries surrounding its Bonza Sur deposit south of FDN. Additionally, there are few assets in the gold sector that come close to FDN’s quality and substantially fewer that would be for sale, making it difficult for Lundin Gold to add a similar quality asset and forcing it to be much pickier than others.

Unfortunately, the only negative to these major porphyry discoveries that could more than double its gold-equivalent resource base is that moving them into production could take several years, if approved.

Hence, while Lundin Gold deserves a premium for a property that continues to give with seemingly unlimited discovery potential, it is a higher-risk name given its concentration to a single asset in terms of cash flow over the next five to six years.

New CEO

As for other recent activity, Lundin Gold announced that after a decade of near flawless delivery, taking Lundin Gold from a developer to one of the sector's most successful and respected producers, the company's CEO, Ron Hochstein, will be stepping down to Vicuna Corporation, a 50/50 joint-venture between Lundin Mining (OTCPK:LUNMF) and BHP (BHP).

Meanwhile, Jamie Beck will join as Lundin Gold's new CEO, whom investors might know from Filo Mining, credited with one of the most significant copper discoveries in the past decade with a resource at Filo del Sol of ~24 million tonnes of copper, ~68 million ounces of gold and ~1.4 billion pounds of silver across all categories, prompting a ~$4.5 billion takeover of the company.

Notably, incoming CEO Jamie Beck also has experience with Fruta del Norte, providing financial analysis and support for the acquisition from Kinross (KGC) and during the negotiations on fiscal terms with the Government of Ecuador. Hence, shareholders are clearly in very good hands going forward, with current CEO Ron Hochstein set to support the CEO transition over the next few months.

Porphyry Potential

As for recent drilling at Trancaloma, the company is having just as much success at Trancaloma as it was at the smaller Bonza Sur deposit just south of FDN. Recent highlight intercepts include hole 290, which hit ~667 meters at 0.44% copper-equivalent (0.32% copper, 0.12 G/T of gold) and hole 272, intersecting ~877 meters at 0.34% copper-equivalent (0.27% copper, 0.07 G/T of gold). Further, Lundin Gold shared that it has now defined a 5-kilometer long corridor through reconnaissance drilling with "significant potential for porphyry-type deposits" next to its high-grade epithermal gold deposits at FDN.

As for adding color to this drilling, Lundin Gold noted that hole 290 has shown potential for an expansion along the northwestern direction while hole 291 (~107.3 meters at 0.21% copper and 0.05 G/T of gold) has confirmed additional copper-gold mineralization and remains open in the western portion of its newly discovered Trancaloma Porphyry. Finally, at Bonza Sur, recent drilling has highlighted the potential for further expansion to the south, suggesting greater strike potential at this smaller but higher-grade gold target.

Just as exciting, there appears to be more porphyry potential on site. This is based on drilling 4 kilometers north of Trancaloma and just east of FDN that hit ~731 meters at 0.35% copper-equivalent at Sandia, a new porphyry target. Unfortunately, these results have put Bonza Sur on hold in terms of releasing a resource estimate with less resource growth than I expected at year-end. That said, the bigger picture here is that there is considerably more mineral endowment than initially thought, with copper-gold porphyries surrounding a mid-grade gold deposit (Bonza Sur) and two ultra high-grade gold ore bodies (FDN, FDN South), and pointing to an embarrassment of riches on this property.

As for the larger potential here, these new porphyry discoveries have the ability to drive a material step-change in resource growth with tonnes/ounces adding up very quickly at copper-gold porphyries. However, with limited drilling and no maiden resource, it's still early to get an idea of the larger potential here, with investors likely having to wait a couple of years to see what the economics could look like. On a positive note, mine engineering work is underway with the goal of integrating this into the FDN mine plan, driving an increase in NAV/share.

Valuation

Based on ~244 million shares and a share price of US$62.10, Lundin Gold trades at a market cap of ~$15.1 billion and an enterprise value of ~$14.6 billion. This continues to be a steep valuation for a single-asset producer, with Lundin Gold trading at significantly higher multiples than names like Barrick Gold (B) that are more diversified and also proud owners of world-class assets. In fact, Barrick’s 100% owned Fourmile Project that could tower over FDN from an AISC margin and scale standpoint, with Barrick materially upgrading its outlook for Fourmile’s potential to 675,000 ounces at $700/oz AISC at the mid-point. Even using more conservative AISC estimates of $875/oz, Fourmile could see production ~40% higher than FDN in peak years (750,000 ounces vs ~540,000 ounces) at lower costs.

So, what’s a fair value for the stock?

Using what I believe to be fair multiples of 11.0x FY2026 cash flow and 1.6x P/NAV for an asset of this quality in the hands of a top-tier operator, I see an updated fair value for Lundin Gold of US$45.10 using more conservative gold price assumptions. If we use spot prices in 2026 ($3,600/oz), fair value jumps to US$47.60, still leaving Lundin Gold fully valued based on what I believe to be fair multiples for a higher-risk single-asset producer. Finally, at $3,900/oz in 2026, Lundin Gold could generate upwards of $920 million in free cash flow, but fair value comes in at US$49.20 and it still trades at a demanding multiple of ~15x EV/FCF for a single-asset name.

Obviously, my view on the stock's fair value is less important than that of the market, and the market certainly disagrees with me today. However, I prefer to position in names with the best reward/risk setup that are being thrown out with the bathwater and where I have the greatest margin of safety, especially when commodity names that are price-takers with depleting resources. So, while I think Lundin Gold is a brilliantly run company with near unrivaled exploration upside among its peers, I continue to see more attractive bets elsewhere in the sector today and I don't see any way to justify chasing the stock here above US$62.00/share.

Summary

Lundin Gold put together another solid quarter in Q2'25 and is well on track to delivering on its guidance midpoint (500,000 ounces) despite an expected decline in grades in H2'25. Just as importantly, the company has done a phenomenal job of keeping a lid on costs year-to-date, reporting H1'25 AISC of $918/oz, miles below the industry average. That said, Lundin Gold will need to see considerable catch-up in sustaining capital in H2'25 and while higher AISC will be more than offset by record gold prices, I don't see nearly enough of a margin of safety for the stock at current levels.

So, while I think Lundin Gold is one of the best-run producers sector-wide with incredible exploration upside across both near-mine and regionally, I continue to see more attractive bets elsewhere today and I don't see any way to justify chasing the stock here above US$62.00.

data.title: Better Home: Sell The Surge With Growth Priced In (Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Better Home & Finance Holding Company (NASDAQ:BETR) have been on a remarkable run lately, rallying 67% from a year ago and up more than 3x from their lows. To an extent, shares have been caught up in the “meme” rally that has impacted many housing-related stocks, namely Opendoor (OPEN). I last covered shares of Better in May when I rated the stock a “Hold,” though I saw upside to $20 for investors with high-risk tolerances. Since then, Better has rallied about 150%, an incredible move that certainly would be consistent with a “Buy” rating. Given how much shares have moved and with updated financials, now is a good time to revisit BETR.

Tech investments are driving results

Better Home is a fintech mortgage company, leveraging its technology platform to speed up the timely and costly mortgage origination process. The company is aggressively building out a technology platform, powered by AI, to speed the mortgage process to just one day. In fact, 43% of loans were AI underwritten, and management aims to bring this share to 75%. As you can see below, it is already approving loans more quickly than peers.

Importantly, these loans are also more profitable, given lower operating costs associated with them due to lessened labor needs. We are seeing some signs of improvement, though financial results are mixed. In the company’s second quarter, reported on Aug. 7, Better Home & Finance generated $44 million of revenue, about $5 million below consensus. Encouragingly, AI loans have a ~40% contribution margin, whereas its traditional direct-to-consumer contribution margin is just ~13%.

Growth targets are ambitious with EBITDA still negative

Growing the size and share of AI loans should help lift margins to improve EBITDA. Indeed, management expects AI to grow by ~16% sequentially. This growth is essential if the company is going to deliver on its hope to reach EBITDA breakeven by Q3 2026, as laid out on the earnings call. This is the first time since 2021 management saw a line of sight to breakeven.

Now, Better is still far away from this, and it will need substantial growth just to become a breakeven business, though this is a faster timeline than my 2027 expectation; I am cautious about BETR meeting this target. In Q2, BET reported a $27 million adjusted EBITDA loss. This was actually $4 million worse than a year ago. Now, its exit from its sub-scaled UK operation should provide a benefit in H2. Plus, we are starting to see expenses inflect. They were down 3% sequentially.

Importantly, the platform is growing even in a muted mortgage environment. Funded loan volumes rose by 25% to $1.2 billion. This growth was entirely driven by strength in home equity and refinance volumes, both of which more than doubled. Purchase volume growth was relatively muted at 1% and is still about two-thirds of volumes. Revenue was up a third from last year, outpacing loan growth, thanks to improved margins. Gain on loans rose by 50% to $37 million, reflecting the better margins on its AI-written loans. The company expects total 2025 issuance to exceed 2024 levels.

Now, aside from BETR’s own market share growth, the other potential benefit would be a decline in interest rates, which could increase mortgage activity, both in the purchase and refinance channels. Encouragingly, we are starting to see mortgage rates come down, and they are back near a 52-week low. My view has been that mortgage rates would struggle to sustainably fall below 6.5%, and while this floor had proven resilient, we have pushed below it. It remains to be seen if this decline can be sustained, but if we keep rates below 6.25%, that may help to bring some borrowers into the market. I would emphasize that mortgage rates are much more closely tied to the 10-year treasury than the Fed funds rate. With the 10-year yield having bounced back above 4%, we may see a reversal higher in mortgage rates, and I do not expect mortgage rates to fall below 6%.

Balance sheet action has helped; further equity issuance would support growth

Better has also taken action to improve its balance sheet. During the company’s second quarter, it retired $521 million of convertible debt, adding $210 million of net equity value. The company retired that debt with ~$110 million of cash and a new $155 million debt at ~6%. Essentially, SoftBank, the existing bondholder, took a ~50% loss on its debt position, which speaks to the weakness of Better’s underlying financial position just a few months ago. In hindsight, with the stock having surged so much, SoftBank likely would have been better off holding the convertibles, though such a rally could not have been predicted.

Better now has $77 million of book equity from -$58 million at the start of the year, thanks to this transaction. It has $200 million of debt. BETR has about $575 million in funding capacity. It holds loans for about 26 days, giving it just about $2 billion in total quarterly capacity. This would allow it to grow the pace of transactions substantially from current levels. Another source of value is potentially leveraging its Tinman AI platform and licensing it to other mortgage providers.

Better’s book value is about $5/share, and with its market value now about $500 million, the market is forecasting substantial growth and margin improvement. Ultimately, to justify where shares are trading, we need to see BETR become a ~$50-$60 million EBITDA business at an 8-10x multiple. That will likely require doubling the size of the business, if not growing a bit more than that. With volumes up 25% from a year ago, that would require 3+ years of this growth to get there, an extremely ambitious target. This is a highly aggressive and speculative investment.

I would take profits in BETR

The company would also need to meaningfully increase its warehouse capacity to support larger volumes. With where shares are today, I would rather see BETR issue equity, take advantage of the elevated price to repay debt, further enhance liquidity, and support ongoing investment in technology scaling and refinement. BETR is now trading at a ~60% premium to where I saw upside and is pricing in a material mortgage boom that may not occur given sticky long-term rates.

While meme momentum can certainly persist for some time, this is still a niche, money-losing business that will need to generate substantial scale just to reach breakeven. I am cautious on its ability to hit breakeven in a year, given ~50+% volume growth will be difficult to achieve, especially as larger peers like Rocket (RKT) are also investing in AI and technology to enhance their offerings. As such, I suggest taking advantage of this exuberance under shares to "Sell" BETR at a price that reflects all the good news being priced in, as I still view fair value closer to the $20s.

data.title: Deckers Outdoor: International Expansion And Brand Strength Fuel Upside
data.description:
The chart below is intriguing, right? While the giant NIKE (NKE) is clearly struggling to maintain reasonable growth, smaller brands such as Deckers Outdoor's (NYSE:DECK) Hoka and On Holding's (ONON) On, which I recently started covering, are posting much better numbers.

To explain this, we can point to some mistakes made by Nike. Among them are less innovation in the most recent period and operational execution, such as focusing on DTC at the expense of wholesale execution. But I believe we should also give credit to the other two companies, which have done very well in their execution.

However, despite this positive medium-term trend, DECK stock is facing a more cautious market outlook, which has made its valuation more attractive. As I believe that despite these uncertainties, Deckers remains a good company, my rating is a buy.

Deckers: Two-Headed Growth Monster

As mentioned, I believe that credit must be given to Deckers. Recently, as in Q1 2026, the company has shown impressive growth in sales for its brands, such as 20% for Hoka and almost 19% YoY for UGG, but this has not always been the case.

The UGG brand is now well recognized for its design and comfort, but it was once seen as a fad for the company, since in some quarters, while other brands grew at double-digit rates, UGG saw much worse numbers, including declines in net sales. There may be several explanations for the company's comeback, such as nostalgia among former customers or rebranding to attract new customers, but in the end, the credit goes to execution, since all aspects, such as marketing, repositioning, new products, and even taking advantage of the comfort wear trend, depend on management's vision and the success of the initiatives implemented.

The second and main driver is Hoka, which, along with On, is one of the cases that I find most interesting in this market. Looking at Deckers' older releases (like 2013), the company did not report Hoka revenue, and this is because while the company already had more established brands such as UGG, and Hoka was acquired only in 2012. After that, the company continued to execute well to enhance the brand and reach a wider audience.

Hoka is not that popular in Brazil. In fact, I believe that most Brazilians are not even familiar with the brand yet. The last time I saw someone wearing it in Brazil was a foreign tourist in Rio de Janeiro, but they're aiming to grow in the region as well. This is in line with the global perception that the brand has been gaining, since despite being essentially a sports brand and making running shoes, Hoka is also known for making comfort shoes, such as for tourists and healthcare workers.

This is also the result of years of innovation (although innovation in the most recent period is debatable), marketing, inventory execution, and partnerships with wholesale companies, which have enabled the company to achieve this level of success.

The image below sums up the company well, in my view. Some successful brands in the portfolio, but concentrated on UGG and Hoka, a diversified channel, and an already significant international expansion, but with much more to come. As for the financials, revenue is growing at a pace that I consider very attractive for Deckers' size and industry, and gross margin and operating margin are not only at attractive levels but also growing.

In addition to its history and brands supporting a constructive outlook for growth in the coming years, the company also has some strategies to drive this. For UGG, Deckers plans to “expand product versatility” and increase adoption by male consumers, while for Hoka, it plans to “increase brand awareness through expanded marketing and distribution.” For both brands, Deckers intends to increase market share in some geographies and categories, which seems very possible to me, creating not only optionality for up-selling and cross-selling, but also growth via markets where the company does not have much penetration. As I mentioned, Hoka is little known in Brazil, while in countries such as Chile, the brand has been investing in the community and has had a physical presence since 2024.

Deckers: There Are Short-Term Headwinds

But the reason DECK stock manages to combine an attractive valuation with a compelling story is the uncertainty.

In Q1, the company delivered revenue growth at a rapid pace, but in the breakdown, it is possible to find some red flags. Both UGG and Hoka grew well, but market concerns arise when we look at the geographic breakdown. International grew almost 50% YoY in the quarter, reinforcing that there is still much room for expansion in some regions, while the U.S., which is still Deckers' main market, contracted by almost 3%.

The outlook for Q2 is for revenue between $1.38 billion and $1.42 billion, which at the top of the guidance gives a YoY growth of 8%, much lower than the company had been reporting. In the earnings call, the company commented that there is currently a “choppy U.S. consumer environment,” which is also evidenced by the greater impact on the DTC channel.

In addition to uncertainties regarding softness in the U.S. market, another major uncertainty is tariffs, as the company needs to import its products to sell in the U.S. The company estimates that there will be more than $180 million in unmitigated impact on costs in fiscal year 2026, so even if the company manages to mitigate part of this and is making efforts to do so, there is still a short- and medium-term risk to margins and occasionally to growth as well, as price increases may decrease demand. In other words, the macro environment remains a latent uncertainty for the Deckers thesis.

Although these are relevant risks that deserve to be monitored, I believe they are much more linked to the economic environment than to structural factors within the company. In other words, in the medium and long term, Deckers will be able to resolve these issues through execution and maintain a mix of top-line growth with a healthy margin, allowing for cash flow capable of remunerating shareholders.

Valuation: Priced With Uncertainty

I believe the market is exaggerating the weight of uncertainties somewhat. Again, these are significant risks, but other companies are also facing this challenging environment, and Deckers, in my view, seems to be punished too much considering that its brands continue to have an appealing growth story.

The forward price-to-earnings ratio is 18x. Of the selected peers, On Holding (ONON), Birkenstock (BIRK), Crocs (CROX), PUMA (OTCPK:PUMSY), NIKE (NKE), and adidas (OTCQX:ADDYY), the only one with lower multiples than Deckers is Crocs, and the company faces several uncertainties in addition to tariffs, such as demand for its brand, declining sales, and high debt.

It is worth mentioning that the company has total cash of $1.7 billion, while its total debt is $300 million, i.e., the company has net cash, which explains its better net margins than its more leveraged peers.

When we combine this attractive multiple with long-term estimates, such as the company's ability to maintain double-digit revenue growth for a few years and gain a little margin along with buybacks, causing EPS to grow above 10% in several years, it makes Deckers a potential compounder in my view, and in the long term, it should unlock an interesting earnings yield for shareholders.

Note that even if it does not surprise the market in its estimates, in fiscal year 2030, the earnings yield will already be 8.5% at the current price.

The Bottom Line

Deckers has already proven itself in terms of operational performance through its brands, building a captivating growth story. And although there are some important yellow and red flags that may signal worse prospects in the medium term for the company's sales, I believe that most are cyclical headwinds, meaning that fundamentals should remain intact in the long term.

Therefore, my rating for Deckers is a buy.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Has The Fed Changed Directions?
data.description:
Here is a picture of how the Federal Reserve has managed its portfolio of securities held outright over the past three and one-half years. The last banking week is for September 17, 2025.

Here is what the chart looks like for the past five weeks.

Starting in the banking week ending September 3, 2025, the securities portfolio of the Federal Reserve has modestly risen for a three-week period. This is the first time this has happened in the full period of tightening going back to the middle of March in 2022.

Has a change taken place in the Fed's monetary policy?

Has the Fed moved from a policy of "quantitative tightening" to a policy of "steady as she goes"?

The Fed did reduce its policy rate of interest this past Wednesday, dropping the range of the Federal Funds rate to 4.00% to 4.25% from 4.25% to 4.50%.

Moving from a policy of reducing the amount of securities in the securities portfolio could be considered to be consistent with the drop in the Fed's target range for the Federal Funds rate.

Remember that in the current policy program, the primary policy tool is the size of the Fed's portfolio of securities held outright.

The Federal Open Market Committee may have decided that market conditions required the Fed to stop reducing the size of the securities portfolio if it were going to begin to drop the effective Federal Funds rate.

Right now, financial analysts seem to believe that there will be two more rate reductions this year and one further reduction coming early in 2026.

Ending the period of "quantitative tightening" could be considered to be consistent with this picture of future rate reductions.

But the banking system has lots and lots of free reserves.

Take a look!

The "excess reserves" in the banking system still exceed $3.0 trillion!

So, the commercial banking system is not in any need of "funds" for the short run.

In fact, one could still make a strong argument that the U.S. banking system has excessive cash balances "on hand" and consequently could very easily overwhelm the Federal Reserve system in terms of a growing money stock.

Oh, speaking of growing money stocks...here is what is happening to the U.S. money stock.

The M2 money stock is still growing at a pace that indicates a future rise in inflation. For the past two years, the compound rate of growth of the M2 money stock has been in excess of 7.0 percent.

This should make Chairman Powell and the Federal Reserve Board a little bit on edge.

With Mr. Powell's term as Chair coming to an end, I think this concern needs to get a little more attention.

The monetary resources are already available in the banking system to support a major rise in spending...and bring on a major rise in prices.

Money stock growth is there.

The economy should keep on growing, at least at a modest pace.

The Trump administration is looking for the Fed to do something in the future to boost the economy.

If you look at some of the figures in the banking system, the money is already there. The money just has to be put to better use than it is being handled right now.

This picture, however, seems to point toward more inflation as a coming fact.
